turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. It's Tuesday, July 21. I'm Sam.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And I'm Alex. Here's the thread we're pulling this morning: think of today as referendum day. For a week the story has been an AI-stock panic — a chip rout that spread from one name to the whole complex and across to Asia. And then, quietly, on Monday, it stopped.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: It didn't just stop, it reversed. Semis were the green on the board yesterday, and this morning the futures are pointing higher — Nasdaq-100 up more than 1%. The thing that was breaking is, for now, un-breaking.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And the timing is almost too neat, because after the close tonight the first Magnificent-7 verdicts land — Tesla, Alphabet, and Intel all report. The very names at the center of the wobble finally get to answer with numbers instead of sentiment.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the panic pauses right as the jury gets seated. But — and this is the whole point — the pressure didn't vanish. It moved.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It migrated. Out of equities and into the oil-and-rates vise. Brent is toggling around 90 dollars on whether a 10-day Iran ceasefire actually holds, the 10-year yield has climbed to 4.60%, and the Fed meets a week from today with a live hold-versus-hike debate. So the mic passes from sentiment to fundamentals. Let's get into it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Two world stories first.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Start with the one bleeding into everything else — Hormuz. And for the first time in weeks, the oil tape actually went two ways.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: That's the tell. Brent poked above 91 dollars intraday Monday, and then faded back toward 88, because there are suddenly signs of renewed US-Iran diplomatic contact — Qatari mediation, and reporting of a proposed 10-day ceasefire.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Which is a genuine shift in what the market is pricing. For two weeks crude has only traded escalation. Monday was the first session where it traded de-escalation odds — the war premium moving in the tails, not just spot.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And yet the physical picture is still ugly. A products tanker, the Kavomaleas, halted and anchored empty near Musandam after Iran's navy targeted vessels. That's 9 ships attacked since July 6.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: War-risk insurance is running roughly 8 times pre-crisis. And the strait is effectively closed — you had something like 10 transits on July 12 against a normal 88. So even as the diplomatic headline pulls the price down, the plumbing says the risk is very much still on.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the trade now is a coin held mid-air. If the 10-day ceasefire is real, you get a fast unwind of the premium. If it collapses again — and it has collapsed before — you're straight back above 90 with a closed strait.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Right. Which is exactly why the second story matters, because there's now a second front pointed at the oil complex — and it's Ukraine.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: This was the deadliest set of strikes inside Russia in more than two years. Over July 19 and 20, Ukraine destroyed 7 Russian "shadow fleet" vessels — the tankers Moscow uses to dodge the oil sanctions — plus power substations in Crimea. That's on top of 13 substations and 4 vessels the day before.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And Russia answered in kind — roughly 40 Iskander-M and Zircon missiles on Kyiv, and a drone strike that started a major fire in Sumy. So this is a real escalation, not a probe.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: But the piece that ties back to our throughline is the target selection. Ukraine is now systematically hitting the sanctions-evasion export fleet — the ships that actually move Russian barrels.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: So you've got two separate wars both squeezing the same barrel — Iran throttling the strait, Ukraine going after Russia's ability to sell around sanctions. The oil premium isn't a one-country story anymore. It's structural. And that's the fuse under everything the Fed does next week.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And it changes how you read a ceasefire, doesn't it? Even if Iran and the US settle, the Ukraine-Russia front keeps a bid under crude.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the subtle point. A month ago, "ceasefire" would have meant the premium drains out cleanly. Now there are two hands on the tap, and only one of them is at the negotiating table. So the floor under oil is higher than the headlines suggest — which is precisely the Fed's problem.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Which is the perfect handoff to the tape. Let's do the markets.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: So here's Monday's close, and the headline is: mild risk-off, but not the risk-off you'd expect from the week we just had.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Give me the board.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: S&P 500 finished at 7,443.28, down 14.41 points, off 0.19%. The Nasdaq Composite was basically flat — 25,508.07, down just 12 points, a rounding error. The Dow was the laggard, down 307 points to 51,839.26, off 0.59%. And the Russell 2000 was the worst of the four, down 0.67% to 2,942.43.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And that shape is the whole story. Because if this were still the AI panic, the Nasdaq is the one bleeding and the Dow holds up. This is the opposite.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Exactly the opposite. Semis actually led green. Broadcom up 1.98%, Intel up 2.13%, Micron up 1.94%, AMD up 1.58%, Taiwan Semi up 1%, Nvidia flat but green. The chip rout didn't just pause — it bounced.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So what pulled the tape down, if not chips?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Single names and old-economy weight. Apple was down 2.14%, and because it's a price-weighted drag it took a chunk out of the Dow single-handed. Oracle was down 4%. Dell down 3.69%. That's what a Dow-and-small-cap down day looks like — it's not a growth-stock unwind.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the bond market is where the actual pressure showed up.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Yields up across the curve. The 2-year at 4.21%, up 3 basis points. The 10-year at 4.60%, up 5 — it was 4.55 on Friday. The 30-year at 5.11%. That is the vise tightening, and it's oil-driven, not growth-driven.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the tell that it's oil and not fear — gold went up with yields.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Gold at 4,068.90, up 1.32%. When gold and yields rise together, that's a reflation and war-premium signal, not a flight to safety. A fear bid buys bonds. This sold them.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Currencies?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Dollar-yen at 162.70, the yen still pinned near record-weak — the carry trade lives. Euro at 1.1420. And crude we covered — Brent 90.34 at the settle, up 1.26%.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And then the overnight flips the mood again.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It does. This morning US futures are higher — Nasdaq-100 up about 1.3%, S&P up half a percent, Dow up a bit. Chips reviving again, and Brent easing toward 89.80 on that ceasefire push. So the machine keeps toggling.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: One data point worth flagging — Germany.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Yes. German ZEW economic sentiment blew out — 26.3 against an expected 18, up from 10.5. The EU-wide read was 23.4. That's a real optimism surprise out of Europe, and it lands right as European earnings are coming in strong. Roughly 87% of the S&P names that have reported this season have beaten on EPS.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So put the two halves together — a rebounding chip complex, a beat-rate near 87%, and a European confidence spike — and the "global risk-off" narrative starts to look overdone.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It does. What actually happened this week is a positioning unwind dressed up as a fundamental scare. The bodies were crowded into the same momentum names, and when they de-grossed, it looked like the sky was falling. Underneath, the earnings kept printing. That's the gap tonight's numbers get to close.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Which brings us to the main event tonight.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: After the close tonight, the earnings jury gets seated. Tesla, Alphabet, and Intel all report Q2 — the first Magnificent-7 prints of the season.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And this is the referendum. For a week the AI trade has been sold on sentiment and a Gemini-delay scare. Tonight the actual numbers arrive.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The consensus bars: Alphabet is seen at 2.90 dollars of EPS, up 25.5%, on 116.9 billion dollars of revenue, up 21.3%. Tesla at 54 cents, up 35%, on 26.4 billion in revenue. And 73 companies report today all told.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The Alphabet number is the one I'd watch, because the whole "AI leaders get punished for any deceleration" theme lives or dies on the cloud growth rate and the capex guide.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Right — it's not the headline beat, it's the second derivative. Does cloud reaccelerate, and does the capex number go up again? If it does, the buildout story we talk about every day gets a fresh receipt. If the guide wobbles, the de-rating has more room.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And Tesla is almost a different company to think about — margins, the energy-storage business, and whatever they say about autonomy.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Fair caveat for everyone listening: all of this lands after the bell, so at this hour it's the setup, not the result. But the setup is the market's entire focus today.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Second US story, and it's a cleaner read on the real economy — Charles Schwab.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Schwab reports today, and the preview is a machine running hot. Consensus around 1.55 dollars of EPS against 1.14 a year ago — up roughly 37% — on about 6.9 billion in revenue, up 18%.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The asset-gathering piece is the tell for me.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It's remarkable — record total client assets of 13.14 trillion dollars, up 27% year on year, and a best-ever May for net new assets at 49.9 billion. That's the retail-brokerage read on the "resilient consumer plus higher-for-longer rates" world — they earn more on cash, and the client money keeps coming in.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And underneath the AI drama, the boring earnings breadth held up too.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It did. GM beat and raised its full-year profit guide — stock up a couple percent. 3M beat and popped around 7%. This is the point we keep making: there's a whole non-AI economy compounding quietly, and it's what makes an AI de-rating survivable rather than systemic.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And that's the frame for tonight, really. If Tesla and Alphabet disappoint, the question isn't whether the market can absorb it — the breadth says it can. The question is whether the multiple can.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Exactly right. The earnings have been fine. What's fragile is the price you're paying for the growth. A miss doesn't break the economy; it re-rates the premium. And that's a much more contained problem than the tape has been acting like all week.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Let's take that breadth international.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Two from overseas, and the first is the European version of that same "earnings without an AI story" trade — Novartis.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: They reported this morning, and it was a beat.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Core operating income of 5.94 billion dollars against roughly 5.31 billion expected, and they reaffirmed full-year guidance — low-single-digit sales growth. The momentum is coming off the launch portfolio: Kisqali, Kesimpta, Scemblix, Pluvicto — plus pipeline progress, including a del-zota FDA submission.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: This is exactly the trans-Atlantic split we've flagged. European large-caps are printing genuinely strong earnings, but because there's no AI narrative attached, the market pays them a value multiple, not a growth multiple.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Right — a Swiss pharma beating on drug launches while it manages a patent cliff is a beautiful business. It just doesn't get the same re-rating a US hyperscaler gets for the same growth. That gap is the opportunity, or the trap, depending on your view.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Second international story is Brussels flexing — and it's aimed at China.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The European Commission hit AliExpress — the Alibaba-owned marketplace — with a record 550-million-euro fine under the Digital Services Act. That's for failing to curb illegal, counterfeit, and unsafe goods on the platform.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the size is the message. 550 million euros dwarfs the 200 million Temu fine and the 120 million X fine.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It does. AliExpress has an action plan due October 20, and it's calling the fine "disproportionate." But the read-through is bigger than one company — Brussels is turning the DSA into a genuine cost center for Chinese cross-border commerce. It's trade friction with a price tag, wearing a consumer-protection jacket.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Which sets up the one China story nicely.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The policy-response shoe finally dropped in Beijing. After second-quarter growth slipped below the official target range, the State Council held an executive meeting chaired by Premier Li Qiang.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And they blinked toward stimulus.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: They pledged to hit the roughly 5% full-year growth goal and — the phrase from the Xinhua readout — to "maximize efficient use of fiscal funds." Markets read that as accelerated infrastructure and investment spending in the second half.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: This is the follow-on to the GDP undershoot we covered, not a recap of it. The number was the diagnosis; this is the treatment.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And it's the same bind China's been in all year. They can lean on fiscal spending to hit the number, but the underlying problem is overcapacity and weak domestic demand — which keeps them export-reliant, which is exactly what's drawing fines like the AliExpress one and tariffs elsewhere. It's a two-speed economy trying to spend its way to the target.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the deflation angle is the global piece — a China exporting overcapacity is a China exporting disinflation, which normally is a gift to the Fed.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Except right now the Fed's problem is oil, not goods. So the usual China deflation anchor is getting overwhelmed by the war premium. That's the world in one sentence today. Let's go to the desk that lives one layer under all of this — energy.

segments.raw: SEGMENT TWO — ENERGY / DATA CENTERS / UTILITIES
segments.label: Energy / Data Centers / Utilities

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And we're sourcing this one from the practitioner newsletters this morning — the CTVC and Utility Dive editions — so let's go deep. What's the lead?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The lead is a groundbreaking that tells you where hyperscaler power procurement is actually heading. Cypress Creek broke ground July 14 on the Steel River Energy Center in Mississippi County, Arkansas.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Scale it for me.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Phases 1 and 2 are 1.6 gigawatts DC of solar plus 1.9 gigawatt-hours of battery storage. The full three-phase build is 2.5 gigawatts DC and 2.9 gigawatt-hours by 2029. And here's the tell — Google has a power-purchase agreement with Cypress Creek covering phases 1 and 2. It's Google's largest solar-plus-storage offtake globally.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And there's a second package alongside it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: There is — a separate Entergy Arkansas deal, Cypress Solar, 600 megawatts of PV and 350 megawatt-hours of storage near Pine Bluff, and that one underpins a 4-billion-dollar Google data center build.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So what's the second-order read? Because on the surface this just looks like a big renewables deal.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The read is that hyperscalers are now anchoring merchant-scale solar-plus-storage directly — bypassing the utility's integrated resource plan entirely — and siting it on cheap industrial land in a steel county. This isn't ESG box-ticking. Solar is being bought as a firming input for a data center. And watch the storage ratio — 2.9 gigawatt-hours is sized to shift power across hours, not just shave a peak.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Which connects to the cost story, because gas isn't the cheap answer people assume anymore.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: This is the Lazard 2026 number and it's striking. Unsubsidized, utility-scale solar is at 40 dollars a megawatt-hour, onshore wind at 37, against a combined-cycle gas floor of 51. Everything is rising on capex and rates — but new-build gas is now at a 15-year-high levelized cost.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And yet everyone's ordering gas anyway.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the paradox. Announced gas capacity is surging on demand-forecast revisions and long equipment lead times — even as gas is the most expensive it's been in 15 years to build. So developers are paying up for dispatchability and speed, not economics. The clean read: solar-plus-storage is the near-term megawatt, and gas is the firm tail you pay a premium for.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Give me the policy piece, because FERC moved again.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: FERC's July open meeting — and this is distinct from the large-load show-cause we covered. This time FERC ordered NERC to file initial reliability standards for large computational loads — data centers and crypto — plus load-registry criteria, by year-end.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So this is reliability, not cost allocation.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Exactly the distinction. Everything to date has been about who pays. This is about how these loads have to behave on the grid — registration, ride-through, the ability to stay online or shed cleanly during a disturbance. It's a whole new compliance surface for data-center developers. FERC also told CAISO and SPP to file a joint seams-coordination report by September 30, which points at West-wide interregional transfer as a relief valve for load growth.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And there's a cautionary tale in here too — the New York cable.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The Champlain Hudson Power Express. 1,250 megawatts, 6 billion dollars, a 339-mile high-voltage DC line. It energized July 1 and then went dark repeatedly on a terrestrial cable fault — likely offline for most of July, during a heat wave. Governor Hochul called it "unacceptable."

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the lesson for the data-center world is the redundancy one.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It's the N-minus-1 lesson. New York bet roughly 1.25 gigawatts of firm import on a single new HVDC tie with no redundancy, and one fault took it all out. Every data-center campus leaning on one big interconnection should read that as a warning — and it reinforces the behind-the-meter, on-site generation case we keep coming back to. If your whole load hangs on one wire, you don't have firm power. You have a single point of failure.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And it ties the whole segment together, actually. The Google-Arkansas deal, the gas paradox, the FERC reliability rule — they're all circling the same anxiety.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: They are. The anxiety is deliverability. Everyone can name a gigawatt of nameplate capacity somewhere; the scarce thing is a firm electron at a specific substation on a specific date. That's why Google is co-locating solar-plus-storage on-site, why developers pay a 15-year-high price for gas turbines they can actually get, and why FERC suddenly cares whether these loads can ride through a disturbance.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the buildout isn't constrained by capital anymore. It's constrained by delivery.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Capital is the easy part. The binding constraints are turbines, transformers, interconnection slots, and now regulatory ride-through. And the CHPE outage is the whole thesis in one cable — you can spend 6 billion dollars and still not have firm power if there's no redundancy behind it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Well said. Let's go to Manila.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Weather first, and the good news is there's no named storm — but there's a system worth watching.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: What's PAGASA saying?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: No active typhoon, no signals up. The habagat — the southwest monsoon — is bringing isolated rains and thunderstorms across Metro Manila, Ilocos, the Cordillera, MIMAROPA, Western Visayas, and the Zambales-Bataan-Cavite-Batangas corridor. But the new wrinkle: cloud clusters east of Visayas and Mindanao consolidated into a low-pressure area as of 8pm Monday, with a medium chance of becoming a tropical depression within 24 hours.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the last system, Inday-Bavi, is gone, and this is the next one forming.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Right — fresh system, still unnamed, still just an LPA. Watching it, not sounding an alarm.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the peso is the macro story, because it's flirting with the record again.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It closed at 61.745 to the dollar on Monday — Friday it was 61.686. And the all-time low is 61.75. So it's within a fraction of a centavo of the record.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: What's driving it?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: RCBC's Michael Ricafort ties it straight to the oil story — dollar strength as crude sits near one-month highs for a second straight session, Hormuz traffic near-stalled, and that proposed 10-day ceasefire keeping everyone guessing. This is our throughline landing in a specific currency: the Philippines imports almost all its oil, so a war premium is an imported inflation tax and a peso problem at the same time.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the central bank did something interesting — a verbal move.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: This is the new angle. The BSP warned banks against using FX derivatives to profit off the peso's volatility, and Malacañang reaffirmed the BSP as the lead FX-stabilizing institution. So without touching the policy rate, the BSP is trying to curb the speculative feedback loop — jawboning the banks to keep the currency slide orderly.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Which is a very different tool than a hike.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It is — and it tells you they'd rather not spend reserves or hike into an oil shock if a firm word can slow the momentum. It's defense by other means.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And there's the politics too — the SONA is coming.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Marcos delivers his fifth State of the Nation Address on July 27. The police are finalizing security — deploying 20,229 personnel, plus military and coast guard reinforcements, around the Batasang Pambansa. At least four groups have applied for rally permits in Quezon City. And the Palace is framing this one as "simple but dignified" — no celebrity directors, no A-list performers for what's his penultimate SONA.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A more austere tone for a president heading into the back half of his term. And a telling one — you go austere on the pageantry when the household budget is the story.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Which it is. Between a near-record peso, an imported oil bill, and core inflation that's been hardening, the SONA lands into a cost-of-living moment. So "simple but dignified" is as much fiscal signal as it is stagecraft.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Alright — that's the news. Time to switch languages.

segments.raw: SEGMENT FOUR — VOCAB OF THE DAY
segments.label: Vocab Of The Day

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 今天我们学四个词，两个中文，两个他加禄语，都跟今天的新闻有关系。(Today we learn four words — two Mandarin, two Tagalog, all tied to today's news.) 第一个词：究竟。(The first word: jiūjìng.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 究竟。我们今天一直在问一个问题：市场究竟在担心什么？是芯片，还是石油和利率？(Jiūjìng. We've been asking one question all day: what exactly is the market worried about — chips, or oil and rates?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 对，"究竟" 就是用来加强这个问题的语气 —— 到底、真正是什么。它是一个书面语，比较正式。(Right — jiūjìng intensifies the question: what is it really, after all. It's written-language, fairly formal.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 那它跟 "到底" 有什么区别？(So how is it different from dàodǐ?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 意思差不多，但是 "到底" 更口语，"究竟" 更书面。你在财经评论里常看到 "究竟"，在聊天里常用 "到底"。(The meaning is close, but dàodǐ is more spoken and jiūjìng more written. You see jiūjìng in financial commentary, dàodǐ in casual chat.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 明白。所以我可以说：投资者究竟该不该相信这次反弹？(Got it. So I can say: should investors really trust this rebound, after all?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 完美 —— 而且你刚才用了第二个词：反弹。(Perfect — and you just used the second word: fǎntán.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 反弹！这个词今天太合适了。星期一芯片股大幅反弹，绿了一整天。(Fǎntán! So fitting today. Monday chip stocks rebounded sharply, green all day.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 对。"反弹" 是从低点弹回来，通常很快、很猛。金融里常说 "技术性反弹" 或者 "报复性反弹"。(Right. Fǎntán is bouncing back off a low — usually fast and sharp. In finance you say "technical rebound" or "revenge rebound.")

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 那 "反弹" 和 "回升" 呢？(And fǎntán versus huíshēng?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 好问题。"回升" 是慢慢地、稳稳地涨回去；"反弹" 是突然地弹一下。所以市场情绪是 "反弹"，经济数据是 "回升"。(Good question. Huíshēng is a slow, steady climb back; fǎntán is a sudden bounce. So sentiment "rebounds," economic data "recovers.")

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 所以今天的标题应该是：芯片股反弹，但投资者究竟会不会追？(So today's headline is: chips rebound, but will investors really chase it?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 说得好。现在换他加禄语。第一个词：bumawi。(Well put. Now to Tagalog. The first word: bumawi.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Bumawi. Ang ibig sabihin, mabawi ang natalo o nawala — tulad ng merkado na bumawi ng lugi noong Lunes. (Bumawi means to recoup what was lost — like the market recouping its losses on Monday.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Tama. Ang ugat ay "bawi." Kapag sinabi mong "bumawi ng lugi," ibig sabihin binalik mo ang perang nawala. (Right. The root is bawi. When you say bumawi ng lugi, you mean you won back the money you lost.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Ano ang pagkakaiba ng "bumawi" at "bumangon"? (What's the difference between bumawi and bumangon?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Magandang tanong. Ang "bumangon" ay tumayong muli pagkatapos madapa — mas malawak, mas pisikal. Ang "bumawi" ay partikular: binabawi mo ang isang bagay na nawala sa iyo. (Good question. Bumangon is to get back on your feet after falling — broader, more physical. Bumawi is specific: you're winning back something that was taken from you.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Kaya sa merkado: bumawi ang mga semiconductor, pero hindi pa bumabangon nang lubusan ang buong sektor. (So in the market: the semiconductors recouped, but the whole sector hasn't fully gotten back on its feet yet.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Eksakto. At ang huling salita: pigilin. (Exactly. And the last word: pigilin.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Pigilin. Ang ugat ay "pigil" — ibig sabihin, hadlangan ang isang bagay na kumikilos na. (Pigilin — the root is pigil, meaning to hold back something already in motion.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Tulad ng BSP na sinusubukang pigilin ang pagbagsak ng piso — binabalaan ang mga bangko laban sa FX derivatives. (Like the BSP trying to curb the peso's slide — warning the banks against FX derivatives.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Ano ang pagkakaiba ng "pigilin" at "hadlangan"? (What's the difference between pigilin and hadlangan?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Ang "hadlangan" ay harangan nang tuluyan — parang pader. Ang "pigilin" ay pabagalin o supilin ang isang puwersang gumagalaw na — hindi mo tuluyang mapapatigil, pero mapapabagal mo. (Hadlangan is to block outright — like a wall. Pigilin is to slow or restrain a force already moving — you may not stop it fully, but you can slow it.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Kaya hindi mahahadlangan ng BSP ang oil shock, pero puwede nilang pigilin ang bilis ng paghina ng piso. (So the BSP can't block the oil shock, but they can curb how fast the peso weakens.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Perpekto. 四个词：究竟、反弹、bumawi、pigilin。(Perfect. Four words: jiūjìng, fǎntán, bumawi, pigilin.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: From currency defense to something much older and stranger. Arts and culture — and Alex, you've got a genuinely wild one to lead with.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: I do. In London, the Royal Academy opens "Richard Dadd: Beyond Bedlam" on July 25, running through October 25. And Dadd is one of the most extraordinary figures in Victorian art.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Give people the story, because it's almost unbelievable.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Dadd, born 1817, was a rising talent — and then, in the grip of a psychotic breakdown, he killed his father. He spent the rest of his life, until 1886, painting inside the asylums at Bethlem and Broadmoor. And what he produced there is astonishing — needle-sharp fairy paintings, obsessive in detail. The masterpiece is "The Fairy Feller's Master-Stroke," which he worked on from 1855 to 1864.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And this is the first major reckoning with him in a long time.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It's the most comprehensive show since the 1974 Tate survey — over 100 works, guest-curated by Nicholas Tromans, who wrote the definitive study. And the curatorial argument is the interesting part: it deliberately pushes past the "mad genius" cliché to make the case for the sheer technical virtuosity and narrative density of the work. The illness is context, not the whole story.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: From an asylum in Victorian England to a very loud summer in opera. Bayreuth's having a big year.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: An enormous one — it's the festival's 150th anniversary, opening July 24 and 25. Two headline events. First, Bayreuth's first-ever staging of Wagner's early opera "Rienzi" — which is a real curiosity, because it's traditionally not performed on that stage. And second, a brand-new "Ring" cycle billed as "RING 10010110," using AI-generated projections as, in their words, a generative visual force reflecting 150 years of interpretation.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: An AI "Ring." That will divide people.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It will absolutely divide the Wagnerians. And down in Salzburg — which opened on the 17th under the theme "Panorama of Love" — there's a new "Carmen" premiering July 26 that's a genuine high-wire act. Asmik Grigorian sings the title role for the first time, Teodor Currentzis conducts, and it's staged and choreographed by Gabriela Carrizo of the Belgian dance-theatre troupe Peeping Tom, with Jonathan Tetelman as Don José.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Grigorian doing Carmen for the first time, with a dance-theatre director — that's the kind of pairing that's either a triumph or a scandal.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And no in-between. Both of those are previews — they open this week, so no reviews yet. Worth circling on the calendar.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And on the page — Colson Whitehead closes out a trilogy.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: He does. "Cool Machine," out this month from Doubleday, is the third and final Ray Carney novel — the Harlem trilogy. It carries the furniture-salesman-and-fence Carney into Reaganomics-era New York, across 1981, 1983, and 1986. Publishers Weekly called it "the greatest New York novel in years."

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And this is a two-time Pulitzer winner landing the plane on a trilogy.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Which is the hard thing to do well, and the early word is he sticks it. And one to hold for next week — the Booker Prize 2026 longlist drops on July 28, with a judging panel chaired by Mary Beard, alongside Patricia Lockwood and Raymond Antrobus. That'll be a conversation.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Quick one to close the segment?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Daniel Mason's "A Vermont Novel," out this month — his first since "North Woods" in 2023, which a lot of people loved. Early notices call it witty and gorgeous.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A good stack. And speaking of things growing back — let's end on the good thing.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Here's the one to leave you with — and it's a genuinely clever answer to one of the ugliest problems in materials science. Scientists have built a "living plastic" that self-destructs on command, and breaks down completely in six days — without leaving any microplastics behind.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The microplastics part is the whole game, isn't it? Because normally when plastic "degrades," it just shatters into smaller and smaller pieces.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's exactly the trap this gets around. A team at the Shenzhen Institute of Synthetic Biology — published in ACS Applied Polymer Materials — engineered two cooperating strains of the bacterium Bacillus subtilis and baked them, in dormant spore form, right into the plastic film itself.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the microbes live inside the material.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Dormant, until you trigger them. Add a warm nutrient broth — around 50 degrees Celsius — and the spores wake up and produce two complementary enzymes. One acts like a random chopper, slicing the long polymer chains into shorter pieces. The other is a patient nibbler, chewing those pieces from the ends all the way down to the base building blocks.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And because the two work together, there's no intermediate microplastic stage.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Right — that's the elegance of it. The material goes all the way back to monomers, not to a cloud of tiny fragments. They used polycaprolactone, which is common in 3D printing and some surgical sutures. Six days, fully gone, on command.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A plastic that cleans up after itself. That's the kind of small, concrete win that actually compounds.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It's engineering biology doing what it does best — building the solution into the problem. A lovely place to end.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A good note to end on. That's The Morning Commute for Tuesday, July 21. Tonight the earnings jury comes back — Tesla, Alphabet, Intel — and we'll have the verdict tomorrow.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Until then, take it easy, and we'll see you in the morning.
